title: Toffee apples
subtitle: Hard-crack toffee-coated apples on sticks
excerpt: Eating apples coated in hard-crack toffee cooked to 150°C and left to set on baking paper. Six toffee apples, one batch of sugar. The toffee sets in under a minute once the apple is dipped.
sourceNotes: Toffee apples are documented in British domestic confectionery from the nineteenth century onward. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: toffee made from sugar, butter, and golden syrup cooked to hard crack stage is documented in the confectionery chapter. The toffee apple specifically (a stick inserted into an apple and dipped in hard toffee) is recorded as a traditional British autumn fair preparation in Florence White, Good Things in England (1932), linked to Bonfire Night and harvest fairs.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten the same day. The toffee softens within 24 hours if the apples are stored at room temperature.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: hard-crack stage, 150°C; the toffee will shatter cleanly if a drop is put into cold water

glossaryTerms.definition: A sugar cooking stage reached at 149–154°C in which the sugar syrup has lost almost all its water. A small amount dropped into cold water sets to a rigid, glass-like thread that snaps cleanly. This is the hottest stage used in confectionery before caramelisation begins. Toffee apples, boiled sweets, and lollipops are all made at hard crack. Above 160°C the sugar begins to caramelise and brown.

content.text: at 150°C for a shell that snaps rather than chews. A sugar thermometer is the only reliable way to judge this. Keep children away from the hob during the dipping stage. If hot syrup touches skin, run cold water over it for at least ten minutes and seek medical care if needed.

Dry the {{apple-eating}} thoroughly with a clean cloth; any moisture on the skin will prevent the toffee adhering. Push a wooden skewer or lolly stick firmly into the stalk end of each apple. Line a baking tray with lightly oiled baking paper and have it beside the hob before you start.

Put the {{caster-sugar}}, {{golden-syrup}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and 100 ml cold water in a medium saucepan. Stir over low heat until the sugar dissolves, then stop stirring and clip the sugar thermometer to the side with the tip not touching the base. Raise the heat to medium-high and cook without stirring until the toffee reaches 150°C; 12–15 minutes. The colour will be pale amber. Remove from the heat immediately and tilt the pan to pool the toffee at one side.
Dip and set, about 1 minute per apple

Working quickly, hold each apple by its stick, dip it into the toffee, and tilt the pan to coat the whole apple in one smooth movement. Lift out and hold above the pan for 5 seconds to let the excess drip, then place on the oiled paper. The toffee sets within 60 seconds. If the toffee in the pan starts to harden, return it to the heat for 30 seconds.
